data.title: FTSE MIB Falls Amid US Weak Job Data
data.description:
The FTSE MIB fell 0.9% to close at 41,608 on Friday, as poor labor market data from the US raised growth concerns on the world's largest economy and pressured equities across the globe.

Sentiment was also down on a stall in retail sales domestically, missing expectations of a 0.4% jump to suggest that household consumption may not support the domestic GDP as it did in the second quarter.

Banks were among the main laggards, with UniCredit and Intesa Sanpaolo dropping firmly.

In turn, STMicroelectronics jumped nearly 4% after receiving an upgrade from BNP Paribas, as the French bank likely sees the bottom of the chip-maker's recent selloff.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1022 ET - Investors are broadly predicting the death of software due to AI, but the industry's struggles will eventually subside, say JPMorgan analysts. The relative valuation of software against semiconductors is approaching a level that has historically yielded strong alpha for software stocks, the analysts say, adding that software stocks typically perform better in rate-cutting environments, which will likely take shape in coming weeks. The analysts also say HubSpot offered a strong response to the AI-induced-pressure narrative this week by saying "we provide the customer context to drive insights about growth--that is not there in the LLM." In addition, JPMorgan says HubSpot now offers a hybrid pricing model, rather than purely seat-based, offering a flexible way for customers to pay. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Many long-time homeowners are sitting on substantial equity gains, but today's shifting market means selling successfully requires more strategy and preparation than in years past, Realtor.com says. Starting the process early--before the peak spring season--can make the difference between a smooth sale and missed opportunities. Nearly half--45.2%--of homeowners have lived in their homes for more than 15 years, with one in four staying put for more than 25 years. U.S. home prices have tripled from $145,000 in June 2000 to $435,300 in June 2025, according to the National Association of Realtors. For many owners, that means hundreds of thousands of dollars in potential equity. A typical homeowner who bought in 2005 has seen their home value rise by about 90%, boosting equity by more than $200,000. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1003 ET - Investors have expressed concern that Google AI Overviews will hurt HubSpot by taking away valuable web traffic. But the company anticipated this change years in advance and pivoted beyond search to alternate emerging channels including YouTube and podcasts, say JPMorgan analysts. HubSpot is now cited in more large language models than any other customer relationship management company and is now tracking when AI generates leads for customers' businesses, which will help the company sustain demand in the AI era. "HubSpot's adept maneuvering should be viewed as a feather in its cap and an ability to see around corners," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0941 ET - HubSpot shares' 43% decline since mid-February gives investors a golden opportunity, say JPMorgan analysts, who give the stock a $675 price target. "At every other juncture when HUBS shares have seen a material selloff, investors have been well served to patiently accumulate shares with a long-term horizon," the analysts say, adding that the stock's current revenue multiple has in the past correlated to a one-year-forward return of 40% to 100%. While short-term demand may remain uneven as companies slow their tech investments amid higher capital costs, HubSpot's innovation and new AI services should drive long-term growth, the analysts say. Shares rise 4.2% to $483.64. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0910 ET - Broadcom's latest quarterly report was "more reminiscent of Nvidia's strong quarter than Marvell's recent more lackluster performance," Benchmark analysts say in a research note, "showing a continuation of aggressive AI industry investment activity, further hyperscaler customer shifts toward increased custom AI accelerator usage, and the symbiotic drivers of high-performance networking solutions." The company's custom chip business is growing quickly, set to receive a further boost with the addition of a new customer in 2026. And Broadcom's CEO, Hock Tan, signed on to remain in the role until 2030, making the company an increasingly attractive investment, the analysts say. Benchmark raises its price target to $385 from $313. Shares rise 14% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0854 ET - Christmas came early for Broadcom, Oppenheimer analysts say in a research note. The semiconductor and software maker's 3Q results and 4Q sales outlook topped Wall Street expectations. But what really stole the show was the company's strong backlog, and its disclosure that it will add a fourth XPU customer in 2026. Broadcom already makes custom chips for Google, Meta and Bytedance, the analysts say, adding they believe the company is adding OpenAI to its roster next year. Of the company's remaining prospective customers, Broadcom says it could add another active customer by the end of 2026. Oppenheimer raises its price target to $360 from $325. Shares rise 14% to $349.07 premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0624 ET - MFE-MediaForEurope is expected to prioritize integration and synergy execution with ProSiebenSat.1 Media in the near term, Equita Sim's Milo Silvestre writes in a note. Following its acquisition of a 75.61% stake in the German media company, MFE could potentially proceed with the merger, he says. The Italian investment bank estimates 290 million euros in EBIT synergies, driven by integrated advertising sales, platforms consolidation and cost efficiencies. ProSieben's revenue is expected to stabilize and post-deal debt levels are seen as sustainable, he says. Equita raised MFE's stock recommendation to buy from hold and its target price to 6.2 euros from 4.7 euros. MFE shares are up 2.5% at 4.92 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0524 ET - T-Mobile U.S.'s increased synergy forecast from the UScellular acquisition represents a significant positive development, ING analyst Jan Frederik Slijkerman writes in a note. The U.S. telecommunication company now expects the transaction, which closed on August 1, to create $1.2 billion in annual cost synergies, up from its previous forecast of $1 billion. The upgraded forecast contributes to T-Mobile's strong performance, he says. Shares closed on Thursday at $252.12. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0443 ET - Shares of European semiconductor companies are on the rise after Broadcom reported better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Shares of Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment ASML Holding are up 2%, while smaller rival ASM International gained 2.3%. Shares of BE Semiconductor Industries, the Dutch supplier of semiconductor assembly equipment, rise 2.1%. German chip maker Infineon Technologies logs a 1.9% gain. Shares of STMicroelectronics, which supplies to clients like Elon Musk's Tesla and Apple, rises 4.9%. Broadcom posted revenue of $15.95 billion for the quarter ended Aug. 3, above a FactSet estimate of $15.82 billion. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0434 ET - ASML Holding is expected to log stronger growth in the coming years, UBS analysts write in a research note as they upgrade the stock to buy from neutral. The Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment said in July that it could no longer guarantee growth in 2026 amid uncertainty from President Trump's tariffs. However, the European Union has since then reached a deal that set a 15% tariff ceiling for EU semiconductor exports to the U.S. The analysts say ASML will provide 2026 guidance at 3Q or 4Q results that will give investors a clearer picture. They expect ASML to log a compound annual growth rate of 20% in EPS between 2026 and the end of the decade. ASML shares trade 1.8% higher at 659.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0420 ET - Broadcom shares surge Friday premarket after the maker of semiconductor and software products posted better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Broadcom stock closed 1.2% higher at $306.10 ahead of results on Thursday. Shares are up 7.3% at $328.48 Friday premarket. The company reported $15.95 billion in revenue for the quarter ended Aug. 3, up 22% on year. Analysts had forecast $15.82 billion in revenue, according to FactSet. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0327 ET - Temenos's earlier-than-expected CEO transition isn't entirely surprising, Vontobel says in a research note. The Swiss banking software company said Jean-Pierre Brulard, who took the helm in May last year, is stepping down and will be replaced on an interim basis by CFO Takis Spiliopoulos. No substantial further changes at the management team are expected after Brulard's departure despite the numerous appointments under his leadership, analyst Michael Foeth writes. "We had not considered the appointment of Jean-Pierre Brulard as a long-term solution in the first place," he says, adding that Spiliopoulos stepping in ensures continuity and that the CFO could be a potential candidate himself. Temenos needs a longer-term perspective and should announce a definitive solution sooner rather than later, he says. Shares open 13% lower. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1019 ET - Many long-time homeowners are sitting on substantial equity gains, but today's shifting market means selling successfully requires more strategy and preparation than in years past, Realtor.com says. Starting the process early--before the peak spring season--can make the difference between a smooth sale and missed opportunities. Nearly half--45.2%--of homeowners have lived in their homes for more than 15 years, with one in four staying put for more than 25 years. U.S. home prices have tripled from $145,000 in June 2000 to $435,300 in June 2025, according to the National Association of Realtors. For many owners, that means hundreds of thousands of dollars in potential equity. A typical homeowner who bought in 2005 has seen their home value rise by about 90%, boosting equity by more than $200,000. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0704 ET - French banking shares should continue to recover losses incurred following a recent selloff, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. Stocks slid recently after the announcement of a confidence vote in the government, but they should recover even if the vote passes on Monday. Shares might react but should move on quickly to ignore political risks, analysts add. "There does not appear to be an easy fix on how the political impasse can be overcome to provide more visibility on the economic outlook," analysts note. After the vote, focus will shift to risks on the horizon, which include the failure of agreeing on a 2026 budget by year-end to avoid further widening bond spreads and a hit to economic prospects. This can hurt banks' performance though their diversification somewhat insulates them from such risks, RBC says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0506 ET - Thai banks face pressure from high household debt, which poses a challenge to economic recovery and the sector's asset quality in 2H, UOB Kay Hian's Thanawat Thangchadakorn says. He favors large banks with upside catalysts, such as Kasikornbank and Krung Thai Bank, with the former announcing a higher dividend policy and the latter being expected to book a THB9.9 billion investment gain in 3Q. Both could also announce share buyback programs. UOB KH has buy ratings on both banks, with a THB192 target on Kasikornbank and THB30 target on Krung Thai. Kasikornbank and Krung Thai shares are last at THB169 and THB25.50, respectively. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0315 ET - Ashmore shares open 12% lower at 150 pence, wiping the London-listed emerging markets asset manager's year-to-date gains after it reported its full-year print. Adjusted Ebitda was lower than expectations on heavier staff costs while its top line was weaker as management fees also missed views, Jefferies says in a research note. Performance fees were in line. Emerging markets demand should be helped by a weaker dollar and concerns about how much debt developed countries have, analysts say. Signs of this have emerged over the past four to five months, and this should be positive for Ashmore, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

2032 ET - IAG may have to divest its existing operations in Western Australia state if it is to secure permission for its latest acquisition from the competition regulator, Jarden analysts say. They think that the regulator's statement on its preliminary concerns was largely in line with market expectations, but cautions that such commentary has historically been followed by significant structural changes to a proposed deal. Other solutions in this case could include carving out geographical portfolios, maintaining target RACI's operational independence in Western Australia, or even shifting to a partial acquisition, the analysts write in a note. They keep a neutral rating and A$8.50 target price on IAG, which is up 1.2% at A$8.755. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1845 ET [Dow Jones]--IAG's proposed acquisition of RAC Insurance is unlikely to proceed after the Australian competition regulator raised concerns about the deal, Morgan Stanley says. It estimates the combined share of IAG and RAC in Western Australia's motor and home insurance market could approach 50%. That is likely too high. "We think the debate starts to shift back to organic growth," says analyst Andrei Stadnik. IAG's top-line growth guidance for FY 2026 — when excluding benefits from acquisitions — is slightly softer than peers. "With less support from inorganic opportunities and fewer reinsurance levers to pull, we see IAG's earnings growth moderating alongside a slower pricing cycle," MS says. It has an equal-weight call on IAG. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0934 ET - Crude futures are down for a third session and on track for weekly losses as the market watches for the weekend's OPEC+ meeting and digests the unexpected 2.4 million barrel build in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA. "Technical momentum appears to have completely reversed and is now heading lower," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Increased OPEC+ barrels should keep the crude oil market well supplied, and the group could decide on bringing even more barrels online in October, he says. "With expectations of a surplus ahead, it is hard to get overly bullish."WTI is down 1.7% at $62.38 a barrel and Brent is off 1.6% at $65.93. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0413 ET - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0308 ET - Orsted's guidance downgrade is a negative ahead of the rights issue prospectus which is expected early next week, RBC analyst Alexander Wheeler writes. Following lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds, Orsted lowered its 2025 Ebitda guidance to 24 billion-27 billion Danish kroner ($3.7 billon to $4.2 billion), from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously. Company consensus is at 26.5 billion kroner, Wheeler adds. Lower wind speeds have impacted Ebitda by 1.2 billion kroner, with a construction delay at the Greater Changhua 2b project providing a 300 million kroner hit. Additionally, commissioning of Greater Changhua 2b has been delayed from late 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 due to damage to an export cable, he adds. Shares fall 1.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1533 ET - Crude futures settle lower for a second straight session as the market frets about this weekend's OPEC+ meeting and the possibility of the group raising output targets further after September. The EIA's weekly inventory report was "quite bearish to both crude and the distillates but bullish to gasoline," Ritterbusch says in a note. U.S. crude stocks rose by 2.4 million barrels against expectations of a decline. "And the fact that Cushing accounted for more than 2/3 of the increase with stocks lifting some 1.6 million barrels should accentuate negative response to today's data by forcing further weakness into the WTI curve," Ritterbusch adds. WTI settles down 0.8% at $63.48 a barrel, and Brent falls 0.9% to $66.99. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2223 ET - Iron ore futures are mixed in Asian trading as steel production resumes in northern China after Beijing's military parade. Despite the temporary production halt, Chinese steel mills' margins are lower in recent weeks, which suggests deteriorating steel demand, says CBA's Vivek Dhar in a commentary. This is likely linked to the nationwide construction activity index from China's non-manufacturing PMI contracting in August, which could point to a decline in construction, he notes. "We anticipate that China's policymakers will announce additional stimulus measures in late 3Q or early 4Q that help China's commodity‑intensive economy," he says. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is flat at CNY783.50 a ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2152 ET - Orica's trading update appears to be a net negative, according to RBC Capital Markets. Still, it is "not sufficient to derail our Outperform view," RBC says in a note. The broker says a rise in litigation costs is unfortunate but understandable. "We accept the decision to take these as significant items during the period," the broker says. The update also provides useful qualitative commentary "for a business that is becoming increasingly challenging to model," it says. While blasting solutions volumes in Asia Pacific and North America appear softer than envisaged, margins may have improved thanks to a better mix of products and services, the broker says. RBC has a A$23.00 target on Orica. The stock is up 0.9% at A$20.94. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2139 ET - The rally in gold prices to all-time highs prompts Morgans to lift its target on Australian miner Regis Resources. The broker raises its price target on the stock to A$5.80 from A$5.00 after baking in higher metal prices. It reiterates an accumulate rating. "Spot case forecasts have been revised to reflect the material increase in the gold price, with our scenario now set at US$3,250/oz," from US$3,000/oz previously, Morgans analyst Ross Bennett says. Bennett adds that he sees "value in producers leveraged to earnings rather than growth." Regis is up 5.2% at A$5.07. Its shares are up 99% year to date. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2125 ET - Orica's projection of more earnings growth across all its business units is encouraging, according to Citi analyst William Park. Orica's trading update exudes optimism and is "testament to ORI's unwavering focus on commercial discipline," Park says in a note. He expects Orica's specialty mining chemicals arm to gain more momentum heading into FY 2026. Its digital solutions business also appears well placed to benefit from Orica's geographical reach and an improvement in exploration activity, Park says. Citi has a buy rating and A$20.65 target on Orica. "We remain upbeat on ORI's outlook beyond FY25," says Park. The stock is up 1.1% at A$20.985. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1837 ET [Dow Jones]--Lotus Resources's A$65 million capital raise looks a sensible move to Ord Minnett. Lotus wants to fortify its balance sheet as it ramps up output from the Kayelekera uranium mine in Malawi. Analyst Matthew Hope had felt Lotus's finances were strong enough to manage the restart of Kayelekera. But he says Lotus is raising from a position of strength because its share price is up some 43% over the prior month. "We consider Lotus's approach prudent, as it will consume working capital in December half of 2025 with the commencement of mining and growth of product and run-of-mine inventories," Ord Minnett says. "Cash receipts are most likely due in June half of 2026." (david.winning@wsj.com)

1829 ET [Dow Jones]--Vault Minerals's surprise decision to buy back stock is a good "back-stop" mechanism for its share price, Euroz Hartleys says. Vault didn't put a dollar value on the buyback. It said the intention is to repurchase up to 10% of its share capital over 12 months. Analyst Mike Millikan notes that Vault's share price has risen some 36% to A$0.57 since the buyback was announced. He thinks Vault could spend A$180 million on repurchasing stock. Euroz Hartleys retains a buy call on Vault, and lifts its price target by 12% to A$0.75/share. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1320 GMT - Tesla's board is asking investors to approve a new pay package for CEO Elon Musk that could be worth as much as $1 trillion over the next decade. The maximum payout would represent a 12% stake in the company, which is contingent on certain milestones and would increase Musk's ownership of Tesla up to approximately 25% voting power. This increase, "we believe was critical to keep Musk at the helm to lead Tesla through the most critical time in the company's history," Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "We believe this was the smart move by the Board as the biggest asset for Tesla is Musk...and with the AI Revolution this is a crucial time for Tesla ahead with autonomous and robotics front and center." Shares rise 2.4% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1317 GMT - Parcel carriers' "seemingly never-ending string of surcharges" shows they still have strong pricing power, JPMorgan says. FedEx and UPS have started rounding up their weight calculations, which could result in "substantial incremental spend by large shippers," according to the analysts. USPS is also expected to raise the market floor on pricing under new leadership, the analysts say. Price increases could accelerate trade-downs to cheaper services in the long-term, but for now, shippers don't want to risk their service and volume-based discounts ahead of the peak season, which gives carriers the ability to keep tacking on surcharges, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1245 GMT - FedEx and UPS have increased their peak season surcharges, but the level of seasonal shipping demand is still uncertain, say JPMorgan analysts. Downstream shipping inventory is currently increasing, "which implies there will be some seasonality-supported parcel demand into peak season," the analysts say, adding that e-commerce spending trends have remained strong. But a recent PWC survey found consumers plan to cut their seasonal spending by about 5%, including a 23% holiday spending cut among Gen Z consumers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1235 GMT - FedEx is unlikely to see its business mix shift to higher-margin services any time soon, say J.P. Morgan analysts. Since the end of the pandemic, the carrier market has moved to lower-margin mix with business-to-business demand pressured amid a "muted industrial backdrop," leaving business-to-consumer the only segment seeing growth. FedEx has leaned into this shift, increasing its volume of short-distance, light-weight packages to boost its business-to-consumer share. "The onus will be on FedEx to prove it can generate strong incremental margins on the back of such B2C growth as we remain skeptical this strategy can generate meaningful operating leverage," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1216 GMT - The early removal of the non-China de minimis exemption won't impact FedEx's F1Q results slated for September 18, but will likely present longer term challenges for the company, say JPMorgan analysts. The removal could lead to decreased shipments and a risk to incremental revenue, which implies that the expected $170 million tariff headwind could worsen throughout the year. FedEx has noted that shipments of higher-value goods closer to the $800 de minimis threshold are more resilient, but it's "seen more demand destruction on lower value goods," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1202 GMT - Tesla CEO Elon Musk would receive more than 423.7 million shares of the company's common stock, worth a preliminary aggregate fair value estimate of $87.75 billion, under a newly proposed pay package, assuming he grows the company's market capitalization to at least $8.5 trillion by 2035, according to a proxy statement filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. These shares would be paid out in 12 tranches. The first tranche milestone is a market capitalization of $2 trillion, and the next nine tranches thereafter each require an additional $500 billion in market capitalization, up to $6.5 trillion. The last two tranches each require an additional $1 trillion in market capitalization. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1156 GMT - Tesla's board is asking shareholders to approve a performance award for CEO Elon Musk worth a preliminary aggregate fair value estimate of $87.75 billion. Musk would only get the full proposed pay package if he creates $7.5 trillion in value. The company says it is at a critical inflection point as it shifts focus from electric vehicles and renewable energy industries, toward artificial intelligence and robotics. "We believe that Elon's singular vision is vital to navigating this critical inflection point," it says in a recent filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. "Simply put, retaining and incentivizing Elon is fundamental to Tesla achieving these goals and becoming the most valuable company in history." Shares rise 2% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com) (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0148 GMT - Japan's top trade negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, renewed his promise to take necessary measures to ease any negative effects of higher U.S. tariffs on the nation's corporate sector. President Trump on Thursday signed an order to lower tariffs on Japanese cars, which Akazawa says will likely take effect within a maximum of two weeks. While the 15% tariff will still hurt Japanese companies' profits, the clarity on the trade policy could also help stabilize the economy and support stock prices, Akazawa says. The Nikkei Stock Average is last up 0.8%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Thrift Stores Were Gaining On Conventional Apparel Before Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: Secondhand shopping has surged this year with consumers turning to thrift stores as tariffs cause price hikes in conventional apparel. But thrift stores were gaining even before tariffs, according to a report from Placer.ai. Visits to thrift stores, which rose 7.9% year-over-year in the second quarter, have risen 39.5% from the second quarter of 2019, while the broader clothing industry has seen only 9.5% growth over that span. Economic pressures and sustainability concerns have fueled that growth, which has extended beyond thrift store's traditional base to include more visits from rural and suburban consumers and higher-income demographics, the report says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Wall Street turns red, reconsiders initial welcome for weak jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street indexes reverse after opening higher; Dow lags, off ~0.5%
  • Energy leads S&P 500 sector declines; real estate up the most
  • In Europe STOXX falls ~0.16%
  • Dollar falls 0.8%, gold up ~1.5%, U.S. crude dips ~3%, bitcoin barely up
  • US 10-yr Treasury yield falls to 4.07%, hits lowest level since April

WALL STREET TURNS RED, RECONSIDERS INITIAL WELCOME FOR WEAK JOBS DATA

After opening higher, U.S. stock indexes started going into reverse within the first half hour of trading and turned into negative territory before the first hour of trading was done.

The Dow was the biggest loser with heavyweights such as Nvidia , JPMorgan and Microsoft losing the most.

While a clouder than expected August jobs report came with a silver lining of expectations for a more dovish Federal Reserve, investor optimism on that front appeared to be overcome by worries about what cracks in the labor market might mean for the economic outlook.

Gary Schlossberg, global strategist at Wells Fargo Institute told Reuters that the jobs report "pretty much cements a rate cut by the Federal Reserve at the September FOMC meeting. It would take an awfully big increase in the (Consumer Price Index) to raise doubts about that at this point."

But Schlossberg pointed to signs of "real caution" in the labor market.

"To some extent it's a deer-in-the-headlights phenomenon with companies. They don't want to let workers go just yet until they get a sense of just how the economy is going to perform but, they're not hiring either," the strategist said.

"Our view looking ahead is that the economy will lose momentum. We're not looking for a recession but we think there will be a growth slowdown later this year extending into early 2026 as some of those tariff increases are passed through to the consumer, just not as bluntly or as rapidly as we expected back in the spring."

Economically sensitive bank stocks were taking the brunt of slowdown worries with the S&P 500 bank index (.SPXBK) down more than 2% compared with a 0.5% drop in the broader S&P 500 . Enery was the S&P's weakest sector on the day, down 2%.

Falling U.S. Treasury yields, however, appeared to cheer up real estate and housing market investors. The PHLX housing index , including homebuilders and building materials companies, was up 2% on the day. The rate sensitive S&P 500 real estate sector was up 0.8%, leading S&P 500 sector gains.

data.title: US FDA sends import alert to stop illegal imports of obesity drug ingredients
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration sent an import alert on Friday, aimed at stopping illegal imports of GLP-1 drug ingredients, used for weight loss and diabetes, from entering the domestic market from unverified sources.

The agency said it was aware that some patients are turning to compounded versions of Novo Nordisk's Wegovy and Eli Lilly's Zepbound, which are not approved by it. To protect patients who use compounded drugs, the "green list" alert will include ingredients from facilities the FDA has inspected or evaluated, it said.

The "green list" is part of the FDA's import alert system, identifying firms or products that have resolved compliance concerns and are not subject to automatic detention at the border.

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: Mexican Equities Offer the Possibility of Diversification
data.description:
The launch of CME Group’s E-Mini S&P BMV IPC Index futures offers an opportune moment to examine the state of the Mexican economy and how the country’s equity market relates to currency, bond and stock markets in the United States.

Since 2000, U.S. economic expansion has outpaced Mexican growth with real GDP expanding 71% versus 49%, respectively (Figure 1). Most of the disparity in growth emerged after 2017.

Figure 1: U.S. real GDP expanded 71% since 2000 while Mexico is up 49%

Comparing the performance of their equity markets is tricky. What makes the comparison challenging is that one can view the relative performance of U.S. and Mexican equities from different perspectives:

  • Local currency returns
  • Returns in the same currency (USD for example)

From a local currency return perspective, Mexico’s S&P BMV IPC Index has outperformed by a wide margin (Figure 2), but this ignores the fact that the Mexican peso (MXN) has declined by roughly 50% since 2000 (Figure 3).

Figure 2: In local currency terms Mexican equities have outperformed U.S. stocks

Figure 3: The peso has lost half its value against USD since the turn of the century

When converted into the same currency, the overall returns since the year 2000 have been very similar, but the timing of the returns has been very different, with Mexico’s S&P BMV IPC Index outperforming from 2000 to 2012 while U.S. equity indices outperformed from 2012 onwards (Figure 4). This hints at the diversification potential of investing in Mexican equities alongside a portfolio of U.S. stocks.

Figure 4: In USD, Mexican and US stocks have delivered similar performance since 2000

Indeed, the correlation between Mexican and U.S. equities, while occasionally as high as +0.8 from 2007 to 2012, has been ranging from +0.2 to +0.6 on a one-year rolling basis for the past dozen years (Figure 6). Part of this may be a reflection of the fact that Mexico’s underlying sector composition is very different from U.S. indices (Table 1).

Table 1: Mexico’s S&P BMV IPC has vastly different sector weighting than the S&P 500

SectorS&P BMV IPCS&P 500
Consumer Staples29.92%5.21%
Materials23.95%1.89%
Financials17.64%13.97%
Industrials14.51%8.46%
Communications Services10.44%10.01%
Real Estate1.53%2.00%
Consumer Discretionary1.49%10.54%
Health Care0.53%9.08%
Information Technology0%33.44%
Energy0%3.03%
Utilties0%2.36%

Source: Bloomerg Professional IMAP

Mexico has a much higher weighting to consumer staples and materials stocks, a much lower weighting to consumer discretionary and no weighting at all to information technology stocks – the S&P 500’s biggest sector – or to energy or utility stocks. Despite the S&P BMV IPC Index’s high weighting to consumer staples and materials stocks, its correlation with the S&P 500 sectors has not been particularly high during the past year either (Figure 6), suggesting strong diversification potential from S&P 500 E-Mini Select Sector Futures as well.

Figure 5: Mexico’s S&P BMV IPC’s correlation with U.S. equities has weakened since 2016

Figure 6: S&P BMV IPC has not displayed strong correlations with U.S. sectors

Investors tend to see MXN itself as a risk asset. When equities are going higher, MXN often rises versus USD. However, MXN tends to be somewhat more highly correlated to the S&P 500 than to Mexico’s S&P BMV IPC Index. This suggests that a stronger peso tends to weigh somewhat on Mexico’s stocks as a stronger currency will make it more expensive for Americans to buy Mexico’s products (Figure 7). Indeed 72% of Mexico’s exports head to the U.S. (Figure 8).

Figure 7: Both Mexican and U.S. equities have a positive correlation to the peso

Figure 8: 72% of Mexico’s exports head to the U.S.

Mexico’s economy may be on a rather different trajectory than the U.S. in the years ahead. Faced with higher tariffs, Mexico’s central bank has been actively cutting rates while the Fed has been on hold, narrowing the gap between the two countries’ central bank policy rates (Figure 9).

Figure 9: Mexico’s central bank has been steadily cutting rates this year

As is the case in the U.S. and many other countries, Mexico’s core inflation rate has begun to creep higher. That said, Mexico’s central bank appears to believe that U.S. tariffs will push Mexico’s inflation lower as Mexico potentially finds itself flooded with Chinese, European Korean, Japanese and even non-U.S.M.C.A. compliant Mexican goods once destined for the U.S. (Figure 10).

Figure 10: Mexico’s core inflation rate has picked up slightly

For the moment, Mexico’s unemployment rate remains at historically low levels at just above 2.5% (Figure 11). This is a contrast to the U.S. where unemployment rates hit bottom in 2023 and have begun to rise slightly (Figure 12).

Figure 11: Mexico’s unemployment rate is near record lows

Figure 12: U.S. unemployment has been moving slightly higher

Perhaps the most striking differences between the U.S. and Mexican economies are in the realm of debt. U.S. debt to GDP ratios across the public and private sectors add up to 250% of GDP, including a public debt of over 100% of GDP (Figure 13). This contrasts very sharply with Mexico, whose total debt comes to just 81% of GDP, of which public debt is 43.6% of annual national output (Figure 14).

Figure 13: The U.S. has rather high levels of public and private sector debt

Figure 14: Mexico’s debt levels are relatively low

Finally, there is the issue of valuations. Mexico’s S&P/BMV IPC Index trades at 13.96x trailing earnings compared to 24.66x for the S&P 500 and 33.32x for the Nasdaq 100. This reflects in part the different sector makeup of the indices, with the U.S. averages more heavily weighted towards tech stocks, which typically command higher earnings multiples. It also reflects differing levels of interest rates, with Mexican 10Y swaps at 7.9% while their U.S. equivalents are trading at half that level around 3.69%.

All these factors suggest that Mexican and U.S. equities could remain on different trajectories in the years ahead and that Mexican stocks could offer an interesting diversification opportunity to capital allocated to the U.S. market.

All examples in this report are hypothetical interpretations of situations and are used for explanation purposes only. The views in this report reflect solely those of the author and not necessarily those of CME Group or its affiliated institutions. This report and the information herein should not be considered investment advice or the results of actual market experience.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1124 ET - Base metal prices edge higher in afternoon trade, with LME three-month copper up 0.2% to $9,908.50 a metric ton and three-month aluminum rising 0.4% to $2,601.50 a ton. Copper's broader outlook remains supportive, with investor focus on China and potential government measures to spur growth, analysts at Saxo say. However, the continued build-up of copper in U.S. Comex warehouses is a concern in the short term. "Given that the U.S. accounts for only around 6% of global demand, the tariff-driven flows risk reversing, potentially adding supply back into the global market where London sets the benchmark price," the analysts say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Cracker Barrel has reverted to its old logo after pressure from customers and the White House — but that doesn't answer the question of where its million-dollar marketing strategy will head now, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. The streamlined logo was part of the Southern comfort food restaurant's ongoing strategic transformation. Brooks poses the question of whether Cracker Barrel's choice not to go ahead with the logo change will have other ramifications for that transformation. Whatever it decides, the stakes are high, Brooks says: Cracker Barrel is spending $600 million to $700 million in capital expenditures on the transformation effort over three years. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1055 ET - Lean hog futures add to yesterday's gains following a brief midweek dip, while live cattle extend their move lower. The USDA reported pork export sales of 23,700 metric tons for the week through Aug. 28, down 44% from the week before and 17% below the prior four-week average. Beef export sales rose 22% to 16,600 metric tons, with the four-week average up 51%. Lean hogs on CME are up 1.2% at 96.2 cents a pound. Live cattle are down 0.4% at $2.359 a pound. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Gold prices rise further in afternoon trade as the latest U.S. jobs data likely sealed the case for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates at its next meeting in two weeks. Futures rise 0.7% to $3,632.70 a troy ounce and are on track for a weekly gain of 3.3%. The U.S. added 22,000 jobs in August, below the 75,000 gain economists had expected, figures from the Labor Department showed. "The gold price has now finally broken out of its months-long trading range to the upside," Commerzbank Research's Barbara Lambrecht says. The rally is also fueled by concerns over the independence of the Fed and heightened geopolitical risks supporting safe-haven demand. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1041 ET - StoneX's September customer survey shows slightly lower yields for corn and soybeans than those seen the previous month. "We'll have a better idea of the crop size a month from now, and an even better idea two months from now," chief commodities economist Arlan Suderman says in a note. "Regardless, the crops are still large, and China isn't buying, which means that storage is our next problem." The StoneX survey estimates a corn yield of 186.9 bushels per acre, down from 188.1 previously. The soybean yield is seen at 53.2 bushels per acre versus 53.6 in August. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1036 ET - The dollar extends losses, weighed down by August's disappointing job creation. The meager 22,000 increase in payrolls "signals a substantial slowdown in job growth, reinforcing concerns about economic weakness," MH Markets' Mohammed Taha writes. The data keep the door open for a Fed cut this month and fuels bets on a hefty 50-basis point trim. "Lower interest rate expectations reduce the appeal of USD-denominated assets, driving capital outflows and weakening the USD," Taha says. Treasury yields fall sharply on the data, which he says adds pressure on the greenback. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.6%, as the dollar weakens 1% versus the Swiss franc and the yen and 0.8% to the euro. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1020 ET - New crop soybean export sales of 818,500 metric tons for last week were "OK," Marex's Terry Reilly says in a note, although new crop commitments are the lowest since 2018 as a result of the trade war. "Fundamentally, we remain bearish over the short term with China absent from the U.S. market." The USDA also reported flash sales to unknown destinations of 327,650 tons of soybeans for the 2025/26 marketing year. Soybeans are off 0.1%.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1002 ET - Grains futures are gaining ground in early trade, with corn up following strong new-crop export sales. USDA reported 2025/26 corn export sales of 2.12 million tons for the week through Aug. 28, up from 2.09 million tons the week before. Corn is seeing a consolidation trade after posting sizable gains last week, Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives says in a note. "A sideways trade is expected as we head toward harvest with funds expected to be buyers on pull-backs while harvest pressure in the coming weeks should limit gains." CBOT corn is up 0.5% at $4.22 a bushel. Wheat is up 0.5% and soybeans are up 0.2%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0946 ET - Oil prices extend losses in afternoon trade, with Brent crude down 1.7% to $65.83 and WTI falling 1.9% to $62.28 a barrel. The benchmarks are on track for a weekly drop of around 2.5%, pressured by concerns over a looming supply glut as key OPEC+ members prepare to gather online Sunday and an unexpected rise in U.S. stockpiles last week. David Oxley, chief commodities economist at Capital Economics, says the firm expects OPEC+ to take a pause before raising output further from the second quarter of next year. Still, he noted that with prices having found some support over the summer, the group might feel it has room to bring more supply back to market sooner. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Crude futures are down for a third session and on track for weekly losses as the market watches for the weekend's OPEC+ meeting and digests the unexpected 2.4 million barrel build in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA. "Technical momentum appears to have completely reversed and is now heading lower," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Increased OPEC+ barrels should keep the crude oil market well supplied, and the group could decide on bringing even more barrels online in October, he says. "With expectations of a surplus ahead, it is hard to get overly bullish."WTI is down 1.7% at $62.38 a barrel and Brent is off 1.6% at $65.93. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0924 ET - U.S. natural gas futures extend their slow grind higher, but with ample storage heading into the low-demand shoulder season keeping a limit on the rally. Focus remains on the production outlook as demand is likely to be a bit below normal in coming weeks, Gary Cunningham of Tradition Energy says in a note. Gas for January 2026 is testing resistance at $4.26, he adds, "but still has some upside potential as physical buyers who missed out on the retraction below $4 step into the market for their winter hedges." The Nymex front month is up 1% at $3.104/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0658 ET - Silver's rally on India's Multi Commodity Exchange will likely continue to be powered by structural demand, tightening supply and rising institutional interest, says Samco Securities' Jahol Prajapati. The U.S. in August added the precious metal to its critical minerals list due to its role in solar panels and electric-vehicle batteries. Institutional flows are likely strengthening, as Saudi Arabia's central bank has invested in silver ETFs, following Russia's earlier accumulation, he writes in a note. Global demand for silver has outpaced supply for four straight years, and deficits are likely to stay given limited new mine development, he adds. He reckons a target of INR150,000/kg is within reach for the metal. Silver trades at INR123,424/kg on the MCX. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Credit Agricole close to settling French dividend tax case with fine, source says
data.description:
Key points:
  • French financial prosecutor to propose fine for Credit Agricole, source says
  • Settlement allows avoiding trial, no admission of guilt required
  • Cum-cum trades used to avoid tax due on dividends

Credit Agricole SA’s investment banking arm is close to settling a criminal probe into dividend-arbitrage trades allegedly used to avoid withholding taxes, a source close to the matter said, confirming an earlier report by France Info.

France’s financial prosecutor (PNF) will present a proposed fine to a Paris judge on Monday, who will decide whether to approve the settlement, the person said, asking not to be identified as the matter is not public.

A spokesperson for the prosecutor's office confirmed a hearing to review a proposed settlement with Credit Agricole Corporate & Investment Bank (CACIB) will take place on Monday, without saying whether it relates to so‑called “cum‑cum” transactions.

In France, companies accused of financial misconduct such as corruption or tax fraud can settle with prosecutors to avoid trial.

The agreement typically involves paying a fine and committing to compliance measures, without admitting guilt or receiving a criminal conviction, as long as the deal is approved by a judge.

French investigators raided the offices of BNP Paribas , HSBC’s local unit, Societe Generale and BPCE's Natixis in 2023 as part of their crackdown on cum-cum trades.

These trades typically involve foreign investors temporarily transferring shares in French companies to local tax-exempt entities, such as domestic banks, around dividend payment dates to reduce or avoid withholding tax.

data.title: USAA Restructures Units and Overhauls Management Team Under New CEO — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Rebecca Ungarino

USAA, the Texas-based financial services firm that caters to military members and their families, is restructuring its business and revamping top leadership as its recently installed chief executive makes his mark.

The banking and insurance company is forming new internal units, making a series of changes to executive leadership, shifting reporting lines, and laying off staff. It is part of an effort to better provide what USAA refers to internally as "member value," serving its community of 14 million active and retired military members and their families.

USAA's chief transformation officer, chief information officer, chief strategy and brand officer, and the head of the life insurance division are leaving, according to an internal memo reviewed by Barron's.

"To deliver on the promises we make to our membership, we restructured some areas of USAA, most notably adding a Member Value organization, primarily led by military veterans, to ensure everything we do begins and ends with the member in mind," a USAA spokesman said.

The spokesman added: "We have a deep bench of highly qualified senior leaders who drive USAA's legacy of exceptional service. In some cases, we have asked leaders to shift to a new area. In a few cases, senior leaders have departed USAA for new opportunities."

The firm has made cuts to its 37,000-person workforce. While it was unclear how many employees have been affected by layoffs this year, people with knowledge of the cuts said some two dozen middle managers have been laid off. The spokesman declined to comment on the layoffs.

The firm had recently hired Boston Consulting Group to help review its business, people familiar with the matter said. The changes to structure and leadership have some employees feeling on edge.

Among the changes USAA Chief Executive Officer Juan Andrade outlined in an email to employees Tuesday: restructuring the technology organization, creating an executive leadership team that replaces an earlier management structure, and establishing the Member Value group, reporting to the CEO, dedicated to better serving USAA members.

The San Antonio Express-News reported details from the memo on Wednesday. Andrade wrote that the changes are aimed at simplifying how USAA works, "clarifying accountability, reducing friction and complexity, and increasing our speed of execution."

The privately held, San Antonio-based company, founded by a group of 25 U.S. Army officers in 1922 as the United Services Automobile Association, is in a period of transition.

In April, Andrade took over from Wayne Peacock, who had led USAA since 2020 and worked there for nearly four decades. The firm also named a new chief financial officer and chief audit executive last year.

USAA's leadership team is in the process of fixing internal controls that regulators have flagged as insufficient for several years. The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency issued a cease-and-desist order against the firm's bank subsidiary, USAA Federal Savings Bank, last December, saying it didn't comply with consent orders from 2022 and 2019.

Regulators ordered USAA to improve how it manages risk broadly, and imposed limitations on offering new products and services. The firm said at the time that it continued "to identify and resolve issues while strengthening the rigor of our programs and processes."

In 2024, USAA generated $3.9 billion of profit on $48.6 billion of revenue. Its net income was up threefold from a year earlier, while revenue was up 14% in the same time, according to the company's annual report.

Write to Rebecca Ungarino at rebecca.ungarino@barrons.com
